evals.prompt: I have a support chatbot that answers questions from our help docs. Sometimes it makes up features that don't exist or gives wrong pricing info. I need to add a check that verifies the answer is actually grounded in the source docs before showing it to the user. Using GPT-4o-mini.
evals.expected_output: A DSPy module with a verification step that checks if the generated answer is supported by the retrieved context. Should use dspy.Assert with a VerifyFacts-style signature, include specific error messages for backtracking, and handle the case where verification fails after retries.

assertions.name: uses_dspy_assert
assertions.description: Uses dspy.Assert (not just if/raise) so DSPy's backtracking mechanism activates

assertions.name: has_verification_signature
assertions.description: Defines a separate dspy.Signature for verifying answer against context/sources

assertions.name: specific_error_messages
assertions.description: Assert messages include specific details (what failed, what to do instead), not vague strings

assertions.name: inside_forward_method
assertions.description: Assertions are placed inside a dspy.Module.forward() method, not in standalone code

assertions.name: grounds_in_context
assertions.description: The verification step checks that claims in the answer are present in the retrieved passages

evals.prompt: We extract invoice data from emails — vendor name, amount, date, line items. The LLM sometimes returns amounts that don't add up (line items don't sum to total), dates in the wrong format, or completely hallucinated vendor names. I need format and consistency checks on the extracted fields.
evals.expected_output: A DSPy module that extracts invoice fields then validates them with dspy.Assert checks: amount arithmetic, date format regex, non-empty required fields. Should use Pydantic models for type validation and dspy.Assert for business logic checks.

assertions.name: uses_pydantic_model
assertions.description: Defines a Pydantic BaseModel for invoice fields with type constraints (Field with ge/le, min_length, etc.)

assertions.name: validates_arithmetic
assertions.description: Includes a check that line item amounts sum to the total amount

assertions.name: validates_date_format
assertions.description: Uses regex or parsing to validate date format

assertions.name: uses_dspy_assert
assertions.description: Uses dspy.Assert for business logic checks that trigger backtracking

assertions.name: specific_error_messages
assertions.description: Error messages tell the LM exactly what's wrong (e.g., 'Line items sum to $X but total is $Y')

evals.prompt: I'm building a content moderation API and I need a safety filter that blocks responses containing PII (emails, phone numbers, SSNs), hate speech, or prompt injection attempts. It's public-facing so the cost of a bad output is high. What's the most robust approach?
evals.expected_output: A multi-layered checking approach: regex patterns for PII detection with dspy.Assert, an AI safety judge signature for content policy, and an ensemble/cross-check pattern for high-stakes decisions. Should use Assert for hard blocks and Suggest for borderline cases.

assertions.name: uses_regex_for_pii
assertions.description: Includes regex patterns to detect PII (email, phone, SSN formats) in output

assertions.name: uses_safety_judge
assertions.description: Defines a safety-check Signature that uses AI to evaluate content policy compliance

assertions.name: distinguishes_assert_suggest
assertions.description: Uses dspy.Assert for hard blocks (PII, safety) and dspy.Suggest for soft preferences

assertions.name: inside_forward_method
assertions.description: All checks are inside a dspy.Module.forward() method

assertions.name: handles_high_stakes
assertions.description: Uses ensemble, cross-check, or multi-layer approach appropriate for public-facing high-stakes output
